# Mexico Legal Document Structure Patterns

**File purpose:** Methodological and technical reference for NormTrace-IHR  
**Jurisdiction:** Mexico  
**Corpus version:** 17 instruments total (15 active + 2 archived) — updated 2026-05-05  
**Index reference:** `03_tables/country_legal_mapping/mexico_normative_corpus_index.csv`  
**Author:** Generated by NormTrace-IHR analysis pipeline  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-05 — updated to reflect Reglamento Interior SS 2025 (MEX-017) as active source; reform decrees MEX-013 and MEX-014 archived  

---

## 1. Purpose

This file documents the structural and legislative-drafting patterns found in the Mexican normative corpus currently loaded in NormTrace-IHR. It is a technical and methodological resource, not a substantive legal analysis.

Its purpose is to enable more stable and consistent extraction of legal provisions, institutional actors, competences, obligations, procedures and coordination mechanisms from Mexican legal texts, whether those already in the corpus or documents added in the future.

The document covers: (1) the common structural units of Mexican legal drafting by instrument type; (2) citation conventions for each type; (3) extraction patterns for key normative categories; (4) linguistic markers that signal specific legal effects; (5) implications for populating NormTrace tables; (6) a protocol for adding new instruments; and (7) limitations of the current corpus.

This file does not contain substantive analysis of the International Health Regulations (IHR 2005), the Pandemic Agreement, or any gap assessment between Mexican law and those instruments. Such analysis belongs in the output files under `04_outputs/`.

---

## 2. Common Legislative Structure

### 2.1 Overview of structural units in Mexican legal drafting

Mexican legislation uses a hierarchical set of internal structural units. Not all unit types appear in every instrument; the combination depends on the type and complexity of the instrument. The following is the complete set observed in the loaded corpus, from largest to smallest:

**Libro (Book)** — the largest subdivision, used only in long, complex statutes. In the loaded corpus, only the *Ley de Infraestructura de la Calidad* (MEX-011) uses this level. Where present, it contains Títulos.

**Título (Title)** — the main thematic or subject-matter division. Used in the CPEUM, LGS, LFPA, LFPRH, LGPDPPSO, LGTAIP, LOAPF, LICal and the reglamentos. In Markdown, encoded as `## TITULO PRIMERO` or `## Título Primero`.

**Capítulo (Chapter)** — a subdivision within a Título (or occasionally at top level when Títulos are absent). Used in all laws and most reglamentos. In Markdown: `## CAPITULO UNICO`, `### CAPITULO PRIMERO`, or `### Capítulo I`.

**Sección (Section)** — a subdivision within a Capítulo. Present in the LGTAIP (Sección Primera, Sección Segunda) and the CPEUM. Less common in federal laws. In Markdown: `#### Sección Primera`.

**Artículo (Article)** — the fundamental unit of legal drafting across all instrument types except NOMs. Articles are numbered sequentially from 1 upward. In the Markdown files, article headings include the full text of the article's first sentence: `### Artículo 3o.- En los términos de esta Ley...` The article text then follows in subsequent paragraphs.

**Párrafo (Paragraph)** — an unnumbered block of text within an article. Mexican legislative technique does not number paragraphs within articles as a rule; instead they are referred to positionally (e.g., "párrafo primero", "segundo párrafo", "último párrafo"). Reform annotations in the corpus identify paragraphs by position: `Párrafo reformado DOF 10-06-2011`. Paragraphs can themselves contain fracciones.

**Fracción (Fraction)** — a numbered subdivision of an article or paragraph, using uppercase Roman numerals (I, II, III, IV...). Fracciones are the most frequent subdivision in the corpus and appear at virtually every level. Numbering uses the "bis" convention for interpolated fracciones: `II bis`, `IV Bis 1`, `IV Bis 2`, `XII Quater`. In Markdown, each fracción appears as a standalone line: `I.\n[text]`.

**Inciso (Lettered sub-item)** — a subdivision of a fracción, using lowercase letters followed by a period (a., b., c., f.). Incisos appear in the LGS, the reform acuerdos and the CPEUM. Example from MEX-013: `f. Servicios de Salud del Instituto Mexicano del Seguro Social para el Bienestar (IMSS-BIENESTAR).`

**Apartado (Lettered division within an article)** — an uppercase lettered division (A., B., C.) used within a single article to group distinct normative sets. Present in the CPEUM (e.g., Art. 2: Apartado A with its own fracciones, then Apartado B) and in the Reglamento Interior de la Secretaría de Salud (Articles 2, 5, 7 use A., B., C., D. within the same article). This is distinct from the "inciso" structure.

**Artículo Transitorio (Transitory Article)** — appears at the end of every statute and at the end of each reform decree. In primary laws, transitory articles are numbered ordinally in Spanish (Primero, Segundo, Tercero...). In reform decrees appended within a consolidated text, they appear as `ARTÍCULO PRIMERO`, `ARTÍCULO SEGUNDO`, or `PRIMERO`, `SEGUNDO`. The LGS includes a section titled `ARTÍCULOS TRANSITORIOS DE DECRETOS DE REFORMA` consolidating all reform transitory articles chronologically.

**Anexo / Apéndice (Annex/Appendix)** — present in NOM-017 (Apéndice Informativo A, B, C). Annexes in NOMs may contain tables, figures, reference methodologies and operational manuals that are technically part of the mandatory or informative content of the standard. In the current corpus, annexes are partially captured in Markdown.

### 2.2 Structural patterns by instrument type

#### Constitution (CPEUM — MEX-004)

The CPEUM uses: Títulos → Capítulos → (Secciones, in some Capítulos) → Artículos → Párrafos → Apartados (A., B.) → Fracciones (I., II., III.) → Incisos (a., b., c.).

Each article heading in Markdown contains the full text of the article's opening clause. Reform history annotations appear inline immediately after the relevant unit, in the form `Párrafo adicionado DOF 10-06-2011` or `Fracción reformada DOF 30-09-2024`. The CPEUM has 136 articles and a set of transitory articles from the 1917 original text.

Special feature: Chapter denominations are themselves sometimes reformed and carry reform annotations (`Denominación del Capítulo reformada DOF 10-06-2011`).

#### General Laws (*leyes generales* — MEX-002, MEX-007, MEX-008)

General laws (LGS, LGPDPPSO, LGTAIP) use: Títulos → Capítulos → (Secciones where present) → Artículos → Párrafos → Fracciones → Incisos.

The LGS has a two-tier numbering system: primary articles (1o., 2o., 3o...) and interpolated articles added by later reforms (1o. Bis, 9o. Bis, 10 Bis, 17 Bis, 28 Bis, 41 Bis, 51 Bis 1, 51 Bis 2, 51 Bis 3, 60 Ter, 60 Quater, 71 Ter, 71 Quater). The "Bis" suffix indicates the article was inserted after original promulgation. "Ter" and "Quater" indicate subsequent additional insertions at the same numerical position.

Fracciones within the LGS similarly use "Bis" numbering for interpolated items: `II Bis`, `III Bis`, `IV Bis 1`, `IV Bis 2`, `IV Bis 3`, `XII Quater`.

Reform annotations appear at the fracción and article level throughout the consolidated text.

#### Federal Laws (*leyes federales* — MEX-001, MEX-003, MEX-005, MEX-006, MEX-009, MEX-010, MEX-011)

Federal and organic laws follow the same Título → Capítulo → Artículo → Fracción → Inciso hierarchy. The LOAPF uses a Título → Capítulo structure where each article lists the competences of a specific Secretaría using Roman-numeral fracciones.

The *Ley de Infraestructura de la Calidad* (MEX-011) is the only statute in the corpus that uses the Libro level: Libro Primero contains Títulos and Capítulos in the standard hierarchy.

The *Ley sobre la Celebración de Tratados* (MEX-003) is notably compact: it has no chapters, only articles (1o.–11) followed directly by a single Transitory Article and the reform transitory blocks.

#### Federal Regulations (*reglamentos* — MEX-015, MEX-016)

Executive regulations under the LGS follow the structure: CONSIDERANDO preamble → Capítulos → Artículos → Fracciones. The *Reglamento de Sanidad Internacional* (MEX-016) uses only Capítulos and Artículos, with no Títulos. The *Reglamento de Investigación para la Salud* (MEX-015) uses Títulos and Capítulos.

Both reglamentos open with a **CONSIDERANDO** section (a formal preamble stating the legal basis for the executive action) and cite the constitutional and statutory foundations explicitly (e.g., "fracción I del Artículo 89 de la Constitución..."). This preamble is not operative normative content but contains relevant legal grounding.

#### Internal Regulations (*reglamentos interiores*) — MEX-017 (active); MEX-013, MEX-014 (archived)

**Active source: MEX-017 — Reglamento Interior de la Secretaría de Salud 2025 (DOF 2025-02-27)**

The complete Reglamento Interior is now loaded as MEX-017. It was issued by Decreto Presidencial signed on 2025-02-25, published in the DOF on 2025-02-27, and entered into force on 2025-02-28. It abrogates the 2004 Reglamento Interior and all prior modifications, including the partial reform decrees MEX-013 (2023) and MEX-014 (2018), which have been moved to `99_archive/superseded_sources/mexico/`.

Structural pattern of MEX-017:
- DECRETO DE EXPEDICIÓN preamble (legal basis, CPEUM and LOAPF citations)
- REGLAMENTO INTERIOR (main text): 14 Capítulos + Artículos + TRANSITORIOS
- Artículos use **Apartados** (A., B., C.) within some articles to group categories of units
  - Art. 2: Apartado A = titular; Apartado B = Direcciones Generales; Apartado C = órganos desconcentrados
  - Art. 5: functions of the Sistema de Salud para el Bienestar
- Competences for each unit are listed in Roman-numeral fracciones within each article
- Structural formula: `"Corresponde a [unit]: I. [competence]; II. [competence]..."`
- Coordination clauses at end of each article: `"[Roman]. Coordinarse en el ámbito de su competencia, con el Servicio Nacional de Salud Pública para la implementación de las acciones..."`
- Final article in chapter: `"Artículo 55/56/57"` on suplencias (substitutions in absence)

**Key structural features for NormTrace extraction:**
- Art. 35 frac. XIX: DGE as IHR focal point liaison ("Fungir como enlace para el Reglamento Sanitario Internacional") — direct RSI/IHR anchor
- Arts. 33 (DG Relaciones Internacionales): international coordination, treaty alignment, WHO representation
- Art. 35 (DGE): surveillance, SINAVE, laboratory network, emergency response, RSI liaison
- Art. 43 (DG Servicio Nacional de Salud Pública): new unit; public health emergency territorial deployment
- Art. 52 (CENAPRECE): disease prevention programs; health security coordination
- Art. 2C: COFEPRIS listed as órgano administrativo desconcentrado frac. II (its internal organization is governed by its own Reglamento Interior, not by this instrument)

**Archived instruments — MEX-013 and MEX-014 (do not use as active corpus inputs):**

These partial reform instruments used the **ARTÍCULO ÚNICO** structure: a single operative article listing provisions reformed/added/derogated. MD structure was minimal (only transitory articles detected); both were converted from `.doc` sources with encoding artifacts. They have been moved to `99_archive/superseded_sources/mexico/` and must not be referenced as active sources in NormTrace tables.

#### Official Mexican Standards (*Normas Oficiales Mexicanas* — MEX-012)

NOMs have a fundamentally different structure from laws and regulations. They do not use Artículos, Fracciones or the hierarchical legislative structure. Instead they use a **decimal numbering system** (ISO-compatible):

- **0. Introducción**
- **1. Objetivo y campo de aplicación** (subsections 1.1, 1.2...)
- **2. Referencias** (cross-references to other NOMs and standards)
- **3. Términos y definiciones** (glossary)
- **4. Símbolos y abreviaturas**
- **5–15. Operative sections** (substance-specific; for NOM-017 these cover surveillance system components, notification procedures, laboratory networks, evaluation, training, supervision and operational research)
- **16. Bibliografía**
- **17. Concordancia con Normas Internacionales y Mexicanas**
- **18. Observancia de la Norma**
- **19. Vigencia**
- **Apéndices Informativos** (A, B, C — may be normative or informative)

NOMs open with a **CONSIDERANDO** preamble signed by the authorizing official, citing constitutional, statutory and regulatory foundations. The preamble also records the consultative process (publication of the draft PROY-NOM and the 60-day comment period).

NOMs are cited by numeral, not by article: `NOM-017-SSA2-2012, numeral 1.2` or `NOM-017-SSA2-2012, numeral 5.3`.

The NOM number format is: `NOM-[sequential]-[subject area code]-[year of elaboration]`. For health NOMs: `SSA2` = preventive/promotion health; `SSA1` = environmental health.

---

## 3. Citation Patterns

The following citation conventions are observed in the corpus and recommended for NormTrace references:

| Instrument | Citation format | Example |
|---|---|---|
| CPEUM | `CPEUM, art. [N]` or `CPEUM, art. [N], párr. [ordinal]` | `CPEUM, art. 4, párr. 4` |
| CPEUM with fracción | `CPEUM, art. [N], fracc. [Roman]` | `CPEUM, art. 73, fracc. XVI` |
| CPEUM with apartado | `CPEUM, art. [N], apart. [Letter], fracc. [Roman]` | `CPEUM, art. 2, apart. A, fracc. I` |
| Ley General de Salud | `LGS, art. [N]` | `LGS, art. 3, fracc. XV` |
| LOAPF | `LOAPF, art. [N], fracc. [Roman]` | `LOAPF, art. 39, fracc. I` |
| LFPA | `LFPA, art. [N], fracc. [Roman]` | `LFPA, art. 3, fracc. I` |
| LFPRH | `LFPRH, art. [N]` | `LFPRH, art. 1` |
| LICal | `LICal, art. [N]` | `LICal, art. 10` |
| Ley celebración tratados | `LSCelTrat, art. [N]` | `LSCelTrat, art. 2` |
| Ley aprobación tratados econ. | `LATratEcon, art. [N]` | `LATratEcon, art. 3` |
| Ley de Planeación | `LPlan, art. [N]` | `LPlan, art. 14` |
| LGPDPPSO | `LGPDPPSO, art. [N]` | `LGPDPPSO, art. 10` |
| LGTAIP | `LGTAIP, art. [N]` | `LGTAIP, art. 4` |
| Reglamento LGS sanidad internacional | `Regl. LGS-SI, art. [N]` | `Regl. LGS-SI, art. 12` |
| Reglamento LGS investigación | `Regl. LGS-Inv, art. [N]` | `Regl. LGS-Inv, art. 13` |
| NOM-017-SSA2-2012 | `NOM-017-SSA2, numeral [N.N]` | `NOM-017-SSA2, numeral 5.3` |
| Acuerdo/Decreto de reforma | `[Short title], art. único` | `Acuerdo reforma RCCNIS 2023, art. único` |
| Reglamento Interior SS 2025 (MEX-017) | `RI-SS-2025, art. [N]` or `RI-SS-2025, art. [N], fracc. [Roman]` | `RI-SS-2025, art. 35, fracc. XIX` |
| Reglamento Interior SS (subyacente) | `RI-SS, art. [N], apart. [Letter], fracc. [Roman]` | `RI-SS, art. 10, apart. C, fracc. VII` |

**Note on Reglamento Interior de la Secretaría de Salud:** MEX-017 (Reglamento Interior de la Secretaría de Salud 2025, DOF 2025-02-27) is the active complete source for the SS internal regulation. MEX-013 and MEX-014 were partial reform instruments that have been archived to `99_archive/superseded_sources/mexico/`. Citations to the RI should use MEX-017 with the format `RI-SS-2025, art. [N], [apart./fracc.]`. Do not use MEX-013 or MEX-014 as active corpus inputs.

---

## 4. Extraction Patterns

### 4.1 Competent authorities

In Mexican legal texts, competent authorities are identified by the following patterns:

Articles listing the composition or attribution of state organs typically appear in the first chapters of a law (e.g., CPEUM art. 4 establishing health as a federal-state concurrent matter; LGS art. 4 listing "autoridades sanitarias"; LOAPF art. 39 listing SSA attributions). The word "autoridades sanitarias" in the LGS refers specifically to: Secretaría de Salud, Consejo de Salubridad General, COFEPRIS, and state and municipal health authorities.

Key competence attribution phrases: `"corresponde a"`, `"corresponderá a"`, `"le corresponde el despacho de los siguientes asuntos"` (LOAPF pattern), `"son atribuciones de"`, `"tendrá las siguientes atribuciones"`.

In reglamentos interiores, competences are allocated article-by-article to named units (subsecretarías, direcciones generales, órganos desconcentrados).

In NOM-017, participating institutions appear in the preamble as a list of entities that contributed to elaboration; competent enforcement bodies appear in numeral 1.2 (scope) and numeral 18 (observance).

### 4.2 Attributions (*atribuciones*) and faculties

Extract attributions from:
- LOAPF articles listing secretaría competences (fracciones within each secretaría's article, arts. 27–44)
- LGS Title II (Sistema Nacional de Salud) and Title III (Salubridad General): competences of SSA vs. state governments
- LGS art. 13: federal-state competence distribution table

Fracciones within attribution articles follow the pattern: `[Fracción Roman numeral].\n[Attributive verb in infinitive or the organ name] + [object of the attribution]`.

### 4.3 Obligations (*obligaciones*)

Mandatory obligations are identified by `"deberá / deberán"` (canonical marker of legal obligation in Mexican drafting), `"tendrá la obligación de"`, `"está obligado a"`, `"tiene la obligación de"`, and fracciones listing what an actor "deberá" do in specific circumstances.

Examples from the corpus: `"la Secretaría de Salud deberá promover la participación..."` (LGS); `"deberá garantizarse la prestación gratuita de [servicios]..."` (LGS); `"Las comisiones... deberán turnar el tratado a las comisiones..."` (Ley aprobación tratados).

### 4.4 Discretionary faculties (*facultades discrecionales*)

Discretionary faculties are identified by `"podrá / podrán"` (canonical marker of discretionary power; the organ may but is not required to act), `"estará facultado para"`, `"está autorizado para"`, `"a su juicio"`, `"cuando así lo considere"`, `"según estime conveniente"`.

Examples from the corpus: `"Las comisiones... podrán crear, por cada tratado, una subcomisión..."` (LGS; Ley aprobación tratados); `"podrán asistir... acompañados por un asesor"` (Acuerdo Comisión Compendio).

**Extraction rule:** Distinguish `"deberá"` (obligation) from `"podrá"` (faculty) meticulously. The presence of both in the same article creates a mixed normative structure where some actions are mandatory and others discretionary.

### 4.5 Prohibitions (*prohibiciones*)

Explicit prohibitions are identified by `"queda prohibido"` (most common formula), `"está prohibido"` / `"Está prohibida"`, `"se prohíbe"`, `"no se permitirá"`, `"no se autorizará"`.

Examples from the corpus: `"Queda prohibida toda discriminación motivada por..."` (CPEUM, art. 1); `"Queda prohibido el cobro de cuotas de recuperación..."` (LGS); `"Está prohibido el comercio de órganos, tejidos..."` (LGS, art. 327).

Note: some prohibitions are contained within fracciones of articles that also establish obligations or faculties. Extract the full fracción to preserve context.

### 4.6 Procedures (*procedimientos*)

Procedures are found in: chapter-level provisions titled "De los procedimientos" or "Del procedimiento de evaluación"; articles that specify sequential steps using fracciones I, II, III in order; the LFPA (MEX-005), which establishes default administrative procedures for all federal administrative acts where sector-specific law does not govern; NOM-017 numerales 5–15, which detail operational procedures of the epidemiological surveillance system using decimal-numbered subsections.

### 4.7 Inter-institutional coordination

Coordination obligations and mechanisms are identified by: `"en coordinación con"` (explicit coordination obligation); `"por sí o en coordinación con"` (alternative coordination; organ can act alone or in coordination); `"coordinará con / coordinarán con"`; `"acuerdo de coordinación"`, `"convenio de coordinación"`; `"Comisiones intersecretariales"`; `"Consejo de Salubridad General"` (highest intersectoral health coordination body); `"Sistema Nacional de Salud"` (institutional coordination framework in LGS).

Examples from the corpus: `"La Secretaría de Salud en coordinación con los gobiernos de las [entidades federativas]..."` (LGS); `"por sí o en coordinación con las entidades de su sector"` (LOAPF pattern).

### 4.8 Federal–state coordination (*federalismo sanitario*)

The federal-state distribution of competences in health matters is governed by CPEUM art. 73 frac. XVI, CPEUM art. 4, and LGS Titles I–III. Extract patterns including: `"La Federación y los gobiernos de las entidades federativas"` (joint federal-state obligation); `"corresponde a los gobiernos de las entidades federativas"` (exclusive state competence); `"en el ámbito de sus competencias"` (competence scope limit); `"sin perjuicio de las atribuciones que en la materia corresponden a..."` (preservation of parallel competences).

### 4.9 Sanctions (*sanciones*)

Sanctions appear in dedicated Titles or Chapters near the end of each statute (e.g., LGS Title XVIII). They specify: administrative sanctions (multas, clausura, suspensión, revocación); criminal referrals (`"sin perjuicio de las sanciones penales que correspondan"`); the responsible enforcement body (COFEPRIS, SSA, state authorities); and scale and proportionality criteria.

Extraction phrases: articles containing `"sanción / sanciones"`, `"multa"`, `"clausura"`, `"suspensión de actividades"`, `"revocación de autorización"`.

### 4.10 Health security measures (*medidas de seguridad*)

Distinct from sanctions, health security measures are preventive or emergency intervention powers. In the LGS, these are found in Title XVIII (Medidas de Seguridad, Sanciones y Delitos). Key markers: `"medidas de seguridad sanitaria"`, `"clausura temporal o definitiva"`, `"suspensión de actividades o servicios"`, `"destrucción o desnaturalización de productos"`, `"retiro del mercado"`.

For the Reglamento de Sanidad Internacional (MEX-016), security measures appear as sanitary control actions at ports and border crossings: inspection, quarantine, prohibition of entry of persons/goods, documentary controls.

### 4.11 Control and oversight mechanisms

Identify by: `"revisión"`, `"supervisión"`, `"control"`, `"vigilancia"`, `"verificación"`, `"inspección"` (operational oversight); `"evaluación del desempeño"` (performance evaluation, see Ley de Planeación and LFPRH); `"rendición de cuentas"`, `"transparencia"` (LGTAIP, LGPDPPSO); articles establishing reporting obligations from subordinate to superior organs.

### 4.12 Reporting and information obligations

Identified by: `"informará"`, `"deberá informar"`, `"notificará"`, `"reportará"`; `"publicará en el Diario Oficial de la Federación"` (mandatory DOF publication); `"proporcionará información"`, `"remitirá informe"`; `"sistema de información"`, `"registro"`.

For NOM-017, numerales 5–10 cover surveillance and notification procedures and are the operational translation of IHR notification obligations at the domestic level.

---

## 5. Legal Drafting Markers

The following markers appear consistently in the corpus and carry identifiable legal effects. Each marker should be treated as a classification signal, not a conclusive determination; context (subject matter, actor, instrument type) always conditions the final interpretation.

### 5.1 Competence attribution markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"corresponde a"` / `"corresponderá a"` | Attribution of competence to a specific organ | Often introduces a list of attributions in fracciones |
| `"son atribuciones de"` | Enumerated list of competences | Followed by Roman-numeral fracciones |
| `"le corresponde el despacho de los siguientes asuntos"` | LOAPF-specific formula for secretaría attribution | Each fracción is a specific subject-matter area |
| `"tendrá las siguientes atribuciones"` | Attribution list | Reglamento interior pattern |
| `"en el ámbito de sus competencias"` | Competence boundary / scope limit | Limits the obligation or faculty to the organ's own sphere |
| `"sin perjuicio de"` | Preservation of parallel competences | The provision does not displace powers held by other organs; requires reading additional provisions |

### 5.2 Obligation markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"deberá"` / `"deberán"` | Mandatory obligation | Canonical Mexican legal obligation marker; no discretion |
| `"tienen la obligación de"` | Explicit obligation | Stronger rhetorical form |
| `"es obligatorio"` / `"de observancia obligatoria"` | Mandatory scope | Used in NOMs to declare mandatory application (NOM-017, numeral 1.2) |
| `"se sujetará a"` | Obligation to comply with another set of rules | Normative remission with obligatory force |
| `"conforme a"` / `"de conformidad con"` | Normative remission or compliance obligation | May be obligatory or directory depending on context |
| `"en términos de"` | Normative remission | Refers to another norm for operative content |
| `"conforme a las disposiciones aplicables"` | General compliance clause | Points to the applicable regulatory framework without specifying it; vague; requires identifying the applicable norm |

### 5.3 Faculty and discretionary markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"podrá"` / `"podrán"` | Discretionary faculty | The organ may act but is not required to |
| `"estará facultado para"` | Explicit discretionary power | Stronger form of `"podrá"` |
| `"a solicitud de"` | Conditional faculty triggered by a request | Action only possible if requested |
| `"cuando así lo considere"` / `"cuando las circunstancias lo exijan"` | Circumstantial discretion | Regl. LGS-SI art. 17 uses this formula |

### 5.4 Coordination markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"en coordinación con"` | Mandatory intersectoral or intergovernmental coordination | Two or more organs must act jointly |
| `"por sí o en coordinación con"` | Alternative action: independent or coordinated | Organ has a choice; coordination is not required |
| `"se coordinará"` | Future-obligatory coordination | The organ is required to coordinate |
| `"convendrán"` / `"celebrarán convenios"` | Negotiated coordination instrument | Produces a formal interinstitutional agreement |
| `"la Federación y los gobiernos de las entidades federativas"` | Federal-state joint action | Health federalism pattern in LGS and CPEUM |
| `"los municipios"` | Municipal-level obligation or faculty | Third tier of government in Mexico's federal structure |

### 5.5 Prohibition markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"queda prohibido"` | Absolute prohibition | Most common formula; no exceptions without express provision |
| `"está prohibido"` / `"Está prohibida"` | Absolute prohibition | CPEUM and LGS formula |
| `"se prohíbe"` | Prohibition | Slightly less common form |
| `"no se autorizará"` / `"no podrá"` | Conditional prohibition (framed as denial of faculty) | The organ lacks the power to permit the action |

### 5.6 Normative remission markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"en términos de"` | The legal content is defined in another norm | Requires reading the cited norm |
| `"conforme a las disposiciones aplicables"` | Unspecified remission to applicable law | Vague; requires identifying which specific norm applies |
| `"conforme a lo que dispone"` | Specific remission | More precise; names the provision |
| `"de conformidad con"` | Compliance with another norm | Combined remission and obligation |
| `"la ley señale"` / `"la ley determine"` | Legislative delegation | Operative content deferred to ordinary legislation |
| `"en los términos que establezca el Reglamento"` | Regulatory delegation | The law delegates operative content to a reglamento |

### 5.7 Institutional actor markers

| Marker | Typical legal effect | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `"el Ejecutivo Federal"` | Presidential or federal executive action | The President of the Republic or the federal executive branch as a whole |
| `"las entidades federativas"` | State-level actors | The 31 states plus Mexico City |
| `"los municipios"` | Municipal actors | Third level in Mexico's federal system |
| `"las autoridades sanitarias"` | Health authorities (all levels) | Defined in LGS art. 4 as SSA, CSG, COFEPRIS, and state/municipal health authorities |
| `"el Consejo de Salubridad General"` | Highest health coordination body | Constitutional standing under CPEUM art. 73 frac. XVI; its resolutions have the character of mandatory general regulations |
| `"la Secretaría"` | Secretaría de Salud | Shorthand used throughout LGS and health reglamentos; confirmed by each instrument's definitions article |

---

## 6. NormTrace Extraction Implications

### 6.1 `mexico_legal_provisions.csv`

Each row should correspond to a discrete legal provision (article, fracción, numeral). Recommended extraction approach:

**For laws and reglamentos:** extract at the article level as the primary unit, with the fracción as the secondary unit when the article contains multiple distinct normative effects. The `provision_id` field should follow: `[norm_id]-art[N]-fracc[Roman]` (e.g., `MEX-002-art3-fraccXV`). Where the provision is a paragraph within an article: `MEX-002-art1-parr2`.

**For the CPEUM:** the combination of Artículo, Apartado and Fracción creates a multi-level identifier. Recommended format: `MEX-004-art[N]-apart[Letter]-fracc[Roman]` where applicable (e.g., `MEX-004-art2-apartA-fraccI`).

**For NOMs:** extract at the numeral level. Provision IDs follow: `MEX-012-num[N.N]` (e.g., `MEX-012-num1.2`, `MEX-012-num5.3`). Note that mandatory scope is established in numeral 1.2, definitions in numeral 3, and operative content in numerales 5–15.

**For reform decrees (MEX-013, MEX-014):** the operative content is the ARTÍCULO ÚNICO. However, because these instruments modify an underlying regulation (the Reglamento Interior), provisions should ultimately be extracted from the underlying text once it is loaded. These reform instruments are best used to track the history of modifications.

**Inline reform annotations** (e.g., `Fracción reformada DOF 14-01-2013`) are metadata, not operative content. They should be captured in a `reform_history` field, not parsed as part of the provision text.

### 6.2 `mexico_legal_actors.csv`

Populate from:
- LOAPF art. 26 (list of Secretarías) and arts. 27–44 (secretaría-specific attributions)
- LGS art. 4 (definition of health authorities) and art. 5 (Sistema Nacional de Salud components)
- NOM-017 preamble (complete list of participating institutions across the intersectoral system)
- CPEUM art. 73 frac. XVI (Consejo de Salubridad General)
- LGS arts. 7–13 (coordination roles of SSA, state and municipal governments)

For each actor, extract: name, type (federal executive body / autonomous body / state government / international organization / private sector / civil society), normative basis for existence, principal obligations and enforcement or sanctioning capacity.

### 6.3 `mexico_ihr_mapping.csv`

IHR mapping requires cross-referencing IHR 2005 provisions with Mexican normative provisions. The extraction patterns most relevant to IHR are:

- Epidemiological surveillance and notification: NOM-017 (numerales 1–10), LGS (Title V and arts. 134–140), Regl. LGS-SI (arts. 1–17)
- Core capacities at ports of entry: Regl. LGS-SI (arts. 5–17), LGS (arts. 344–367 on sanidad internacional)
- National IHR Focal Point: LOAPF art. 39 (SSA attributions), LGS art. 4
- Emergency powers: LGS (Consejo de Salubridad General powers), CPEUM art. 73 frac. XVI

Use the `legal_function` field from the corpus index to pre-filter relevant provisions. Prioritize: `regulates health governance`, `distributes competences`, `creates institutional mandates`, `regulates emergency powers`, and `incorporates or governs international commitments`.

### 6.4 `mexico_pandemic_agreement_mapping.csv`

Pandemic agreement mapping follows the same logic as IHR mapping. Additional provisions relevant to this framework include:

- Treaty reception: LSCelTrat and LATratEcon (MEX-003, MEX-001)
- Health financing: LFPRH (MEX-006), Ley de Planeación (MEX-010)
- Data sharing and transparency: LGPDPPSO (MEX-007), LGTAIP (MEX-008)
- Health products and NOM governance: LICal (MEX-011), LGS (Titles X–XI)
- Intersectoral planning: Ley de Planeación (MEX-010), LOAPF (MEX-009)

When cross-referencing, note that the pandemic agreement framework may engage provisions across multiple instruments simultaneously. The `sector` and `subsector` fields in the corpus index support multi-instrument queries.

---

## 7. Update Protocol

When adding a new legal instrument to the NormTrace-IHR Mexico corpus, follow these steps in order:

**Step 1 — Place files in the correct directories:**
- Markdown version: `01_sources/mexico/md/[filename].md`
- Metadata file: `01_sources/mexico/metadata/[filename]_metadata.yml`
- Filename convention: `[descriptive_name]_[year].md` (underscores, no spaces, lowercase). For reform instruments, include `reforma_` prefix and the DOF date.

**Step 2 — Update the corpus index:**
- Open `03_tables/country_legal_mapping/mexico_normative_corpus_index.csv`
- Add a new row with the next sequential `norm_id` (MEX-017, MEX-018, etc.)
- Complete all 25 fields. Use `TBD_REVIEW` for any field that cannot be populated from available sources. Do not leave any field blank.

**Step 3 — Register the last reform date:**
- Confirm the version date (date of last published reform or original publication if unamended)
- Record `publication_date`, `last_amendment_date`, and `version_date` separately
- Verify against the official DOF or Cámara de Diputados consolidated text

**Step 4 — Identify internal structure:**
- Scan the Markdown headings to detect which structural units are present (Libros, Títulos, Capítulos, Secciones, Artículos, Fracciones, Numerales, Apéndices)
- Record in `structure_detected` (Yes / Partial / No) and `main_internal_units`
- If the source is a `.doc` conversion, mark `structure_detected = Partial` and inspect for encoding artifacts

**Step 5 — Flag for manual review where applicable:**

Set `requires_manual_review = Yes` if any of the following apply:
- The MD conversion shows encoding problems (accent marks missing, special characters lost)
- The instrument is a reform decree that does not contain the full text of the underlying regulation
- The normative hierarchy position involves doctrinal complexity or depends on SCJN jurisprudence
- The instrument predates IHR 2005 by more than 10 years and has not been updated (risk of obsolescence)
- The structure_detected is Partial or No

**Step 6 — Do not overwrite prior analysis:**
- If this file needs updating, add new patterns under the relevant section with a dated note (e.g., `> Updated 2026-08-01: new pattern detected in Reglamento X`)
- If provision analysis tables under `04_outputs/` already contain entries from previously loaded instruments, append new entries; do not modify existing rows unless an error has been identified
- Version control for substantive documents: create a copy with a datestamp suffix before making material changes

---

## 8. Limitations

**The patterns documented here are preliminary and corpus-bound.** They derive exclusively from the 16 instruments loaded as of the date of this file. The Mexican legal system contains hundreds of federal laws, regulations, NOMs, circulares, acuerdos and administrative instruments relevant to health governance, public administration and international law. The patterns observed may not be representative of all Mexican legal drafting practice.

**Instrument type coverage is uneven.** The current active corpus includes one constitution, three general laws, seven federal or organic laws, two executive regulations under the LGS, one NOM, one complete internal regulation (MEX-017, Reglamento Interior SS 2025), and one international instrument in domestic translation. Two previously listed partial reform instruments (MEX-013, MEX-014) have been archived and are no longer active corpus inputs. Not yet represented: complete reglamentos interiores for COFEPRIS and other deconcentrated bodies, lineamientos, programas, manuales, circulares, presupuesto de egresos, jurisprudencia (SCJN), and tesis aisladas. Patterns for these missing instrument types cannot be inferred from the current corpus.

**Conversion quality varies.** All instruments were converted from PDF or DOC source files to Markdown. The conversion process may have introduced errors, lost tables, misaligned headings, dropped special characters or rendered annexes incompletely. Documents converted from `.doc` files (MEX-013, MEX-014) show visible encoding artifacts and should be treated with particular caution. Before any legal coding, provisions should be verified against the official DOF or Cámara de Diputados source.

**Inline reform annotations are not operative text.** Phrases such as `Fracción reformada DOF 14-01-2013` or `Artículo adicionado DOF 04-12-2013` are editorial annotations inserted into the consolidated text by the Cámara de Diputados. They are not part of the operative normative content and must be excluded from provision extraction.

**This file does not substitute legal review.** The extraction patterns and linguistic markers described here are analytical heuristics. They reduce the cost of initial text processing but do not replace expert legal interpretation. Competence attributions, obligations, discretionary powers and prohibitions may interact in complex ways that require reading across multiple provisions, instruments and levels of the normative hierarchy.

**Normative hierarchy is functionally assigned.** The `normative_hierarchy` field in the corpus index reflects NormTrace's functional classification for analytical purposes, not a definitive thesis on Mexican legal hierarchy. The relationship between the CPEUM and international human rights treaties after the 2011 Art. 1 reform, the rank of leyes generales relative to leyes federales, and the precise position of the Consejo de Salubridad General's resolutions are matters of ongoing doctrinal debate and SCJN jurisprudence. Where these questions arise, `requires_manual_review = Yes` has been flagged in the corpus index.
